---
title: "Ongoing Housing Stress and Cognitive Decline"
subtitle: "Evidence from the Health and Retirement Study"
author: "Takashi Iida"
date: today
format: pptx
---

## 1 in 3 Older Americans Face Housing Insecurity

- WHO: housing = intermediary determinant of health
- Affordability · Suitability · Security — when unmet → harm
- >700,000 experienced homelessness in 2024
- Older adults face particular vulnerability

::: {.notes}
Housing is not just a shelter issue — it's a health issue. The WHO classifies it as an intermediary determinant of health. In the US, housing insecurity is widespread and growing among older adults.
:::

---

## Housing Stress May Accelerate Memory Decline

**Prior evidence links housing to cognition, but gaps remain**

- Housing stress → chronic psychological stress → adverse health
- Foreclosure linked to faster cognitive decline in older adults
- Two domains especially relevant to Alzheimer's disease:
  - **Episodic memory** — early AD marker (word recall)
  - **Working memory** — central executive function (serial 7's)
- Women may experience stronger stress-related memory decline

::: {.notes}
Emerging evidence suggests housing stress specifically affects memory. Two domains are particularly important for Alzheimer's research: episodic memory (word recall) and working memory (serial 7's). Sex differences in stress-related cognitive decline are also documented.
:::

---

## Research Questions

**1.** Does **ongoing housing stress** accelerate decline in episodic and working memory among adults 50+?

**2.** Does **sex** modify this association?

*Hypothesis: Housing stress accelerates cognitive decline, with stronger effects in women*

::: {.notes}
These two questions guide our study. We focus on ongoing, chronic housing stress (lasting ≥12 months) and examine both episodic and working memory separately.
:::

---

## Methods

**Health and Retirement Study (HRS)**

- Nationally representative cohort of U.S. adults aged 50+
- Baseline: Wave 8 (2006) · Follow-up: Waves 10 & 12 (2008–2012)
- **n = 7,097** (excluded memory-disease diagnoses at baseline)

**Exposure** — Ongoing housing stress: self-reported problem lasting ≥12 months

**Outcomes** — Episodic memory z-score (word recall) · Working memory z-score (serial 7's)

**Analysis** — Individual fixed-effects models (adjusted for age, income, comorbidity)

::: {.notes}
We used wave 8 as baseline because that's when HRS began collecting housing stress data. Fixed-effects models exploit within-person change over time, controlling for all time-invariant characteristics.
:::

---

## Table 1 — Baseline Characteristics (Wave 8, 2006)

| | Housing Stress | No Housing Stress |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **n** | **732** | **6,365** |
| Age, mean (SD) | 65 (10) | 68 (11) |
| Female, % | 62% | 59% |
| Less than HS, % | 28% | 16% |
| College+, % | 13% | 23% |
| Median income | $25,050 | $42,540 |
| Current smoker, % | 23% | 12% |
| Comorbidity index (0–8) | 2.26 | 1.86 |
| Immediate recall (0–10) | 5.23 | 5.57 |
| Delayed recall (0–10) | 4.05 | 4.46 |
| Serial 7's (0–5) | 3.08 | 3.70 |

::: {.notes}
These are baseline characteristics before any imputation. The two groups differ on many dimensions — this motivates the fixed-effects design.
:::

---

## Housing Stress Group: Systematically Disadvantaged

**Socioeconomic** — lower education, ~40% lower income, more smoking

**Health** — higher comorbidity burden (2.26 vs 1.86)

**Cognition** — lower scores across all 3 tests at baseline

→ Fixed-effects design controls for time-invariant confounders

::: {.notes}
The housing stress group is younger but has a worse health and socioeconomic profile. Crucially, they also start with lower cognitive scores. This baseline difference is exactly why we use fixed-effects — we compare each person to themselves over time.
:::

---

## Next Steps

**Main analysis (in progress)**

- Fixed-effects model results: episodic memory & working memory
- Sex-stratified analysis: do effects differ in women vs. men?

**Planned outputs**

- Forest plot of effect estimates
- Full manuscript submission

::: {.notes}
The fixed-effects results and sex-stratified analysis are the core of what's coming. We expect to see whether within-person changes in housing stress predict within-person changes in cognition.
:::
